chunk_summary: This section discusses prioritization, prescribing, and informed consent for donor human milk (DHM), emphasizing allocation to high-risk infants, safety protocols, and transparent triage during supply shortages.
learning_objectives: Understand the prioritization criteria for donor human milk allocation in neonatal care.
Learn the prescribing and informed consent requirements for DHM use.
Explore operational strategies for triaging DHM during supply shortages.
Recognize safety protocols for administering DHM, including traceability and labeling.
key_facts: Pasteurized DHM is prioritized for extremely preterm (<32 weeks) or extremely low birth weight (<1500 g) infants.
High-risk groups include neonates recovering from NEC or post-gastrointestinal surgery.
Parental consent must document screening, pasteurization, and residual risks of DHM.
DHM bottles are dispensed in patient-specific aliquots with batch identifiers and expiry dates.
Transparent triage criteria are essential during DHM shortages to reduce inequity.
Formula may be used for larger, stable infants after a risk–benefit discussion.
reference_quote: Pasteurized DHM is a limited clinical resource and should be allocated to infants with the greatest expected benefit. (AAP, 2017)
document_key_topics: Donor milk prioritization for high-risk infants
Prescribing and informed consent for DHM
